transcript:
[00:00:01] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Alright, we're recording. Let me just — okay, cool. Good morning everyone. Happy Monday. Pipeline review time, let's get into it.

[00:00:08] Sarah Kim (Senior AE): Morning.

[00:00:09] Tom Brennan (AE): Hey. Morning.

[00:00:11] Leah Ruiz (Sales Ops): Hi all. I've got the dashboard pulled up whenever you're ready, Diana.

[00:00:15] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Perfect. So before we dive into individual deals, let me just set the table here. We need two-fifty to hit the Q2 number. That's the baseline. Leah, where are we sitting right now on committed versus probable?

[00:00:28] Leah Ruiz (Sales Ops): So committed right now is zero, technically. Nothing's signed for Q2 yet. Probable — and I'm being generous with that label — we've got about a hundred and forty-eight K across three deals. At-risk bucket is another one sixty-five. So if everything closes, we're over target, but, um, you know how that goes.

[00:00:49] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Yeah, "if everything closes" is the most dangerous phrase in sales. Okay, let's go deal by deal. Sarah, let's start with NovaBuild. That's the big one on your side.

[00:01:00] Sarah Kim (Senior AE): Yeah, so NovaBuild Construction. Eighty-five K for the full website redesign plus a two K a month retainer. Discovery went really well last week. I spent about ninety minutes with Marcus — he's their VP of Marketing — walking through their current site, pain points, the whole thing.

[00:01:17] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): And Marcus is your champion?

[00:01:19] Sarah Kim (Senior AE): He is. He's been driving this internally. He actually came to us — found us through the Ridgeline Construction case study we published in January. So he's already pre-sold on our work in the construction vertical.

[00:01:32] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Nice. Okay, so where does it stand right now?

[00:01:35] Sarah Kim (Senior AE): Demo is scheduled for next Tuesday. And this is the big one — I've got the CEO, the CTO, and their HR Director all confirmed.

[00:01:44] Tom Brennan (AE): HR Director? That's interesting.

[00:01:46] Sarah Kim (Senior AE): Yeah, so they're thinking about recruitment as a big function of the new site. They're trying to hire like crazy for field crews, and their careers page is apparently just — it's a disaster. Marcus said people literally can't figure out how to apply.

[00:01:59] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Got it. So there's a talent acquisition angle too, which is why HR is in the room.

[00:02:04] Sarah Kim (Senior AE): Exactly.

[00:02:06] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Okay. Talk to me about the budget conversation, because I noticed something in the notes. Marcus originally said thirty to fifty K?

[00:02:14] Sarah Kim (Senior AE): Yeah, so — okay, so initially when we first talked, he threw out thirty to fifty. But that was before discovery. Before he understood the full scope of what they actually need. Once I walked him through everything — the custom project portfolio section, the recruitment platform integration, the vendor portal, headless CMS setup — he understood why it's eighty-five.

[00:02:36] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Did he say he understood, or did he say he could get eighty-five approved?

[00:02:41] Sarah Kim (Senior AE): He — um, he said it made sense. He didn't push back on the number.

[00:02:47] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Okay, but that's not the same thing, right? Not pushing back and having budget authority are two very different things. Have you had the explicit conversation of, "Marcus, can you get eighty-five K approved, and what does that process look like?"

[00:03:00] Sarah Kim (Senior AE): Not in those exact words, no. But he did say the CEO is the final sign-off and that's why she's coming to the demo.

[00:03:08] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Alright, so here's what I want to flag. We've got a champion who initially anchored at thirty to fifty and we're coming in at nearly double the high end of that range. The CEO is coming to the demo, which is great, but if Tuesday is the first time she's hearing eighty-five K, that's a problem.

[00:03:24] Sarah Kim (Senior AE): Right.

[00:03:25] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Is there any way to get Marcus to socialize the number before Tuesday? Like, even a rough range. Just so there's no sticker shock in the room.

[00:03:33] Sarah Kim (Senior AE): Yeah. Yeah, that's a good call. I'll reach out to him today. Maybe frame it as, "Hey, I want to make sure we're all on the same page going into the demo, here's the investment range we're looking at."

[00:03:44] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Perfect. And what's their timeline driver? There's a conference, right?

[00:03:48] Sarah Kim (Senior AE): BuildCon. It's in — Leah, do you have the exact date?

[00:03:52] Leah Ruiz (Sales Ops): July twenty-second through the twenty-fourth. So about four months out.

[00:03:57] Sarah Kim (Senior AE): Right, so Marcus wants the new site live before BuildCon because they're doing a big brand push. New site, new collateral, the whole thing. So there's a real deadline. This isn't one of those "we'll get to it eventually" projects.

[00:04:10] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): That's our leverage. Four months is tight for an eighty-five K build, honestly. If they drag on the decision, they risk missing their own deadline.

[00:04:19] Sarah Kim (Senior AE): Exactly. And I plan to bring that up in the demo. Like, "Here's the project timeline working backward from BuildCon."

[00:04:25] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Good. I like that. Creates urgency without being pushy. Alright, so action items on NovaBuild — Sarah, you're going to get Marcus to pre-sell the budget number before Tuesday. And then just nail the demo. You need anything from me on that? Want me in the room?

[00:04:40] Sarah Kim (Senior AE): Um, I think I'm good? Unless you think — I mean, having a VP there adds credibility, but I also don't want it to feel like we're bringing the whole cavalry for an eighty-five K deal.

[00:04:51] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Fair point. Let's play it by ear. If Marcus comes back and says the CEO needs more convincing, I'll jump in. Otherwise, you've got it.

[00:04:59] Sarah Kim (Senior AE): Sounds good.

[00:05:02] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Okay. Tom, let's go to Meridian Health Partners.

[00:05:07] Tom Brennan (AE): Yeah, so Meridian. Hundred and twenty K. Custom patient portal plus a web app. This is — look, this is a great deal. It's just stuck in legal right now.

[00:05:18] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): It's been stuck in legal for three weeks, Tom.

[00:05:21] Tom Brennan (AE): I know, I know. Their compliance team is being — they're being really thorough on the HIPAA stuff. Which, I mean, fair enough, right? It's healthcare. They have to be careful.

[00:05:31] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Sure. But three weeks of silence from legal isn't "being thorough." That's a stall. What's the actual holdup? Have they given you specific objections?

[00:05:41] Tom Brennan (AE): So the last I heard, their compliance officer — Karen something — she wanted to see our HIPAA compliance documentation. Our data handling procedures, where we host, encryption standards, all of that. I sent over our security doc and our SOC 2 summary two weeks ago.

[00:05:58] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): And?

[00:06:00] Tom Brennan (AE): And... I haven't heard back. But that's just legal being legal. You know how compliance teams are. They take forever on everything.

[00:06:08] Leah Ruiz (Sales Ops): Tom, can I jump in? When was the last time you actually spoke with Dr. Patel?

[00:06:14] Tom Brennan (AE): Uh, which — you mean Priya? The CTO?

[00:06:17] Leah Ruiz (Sales Ops): Yeah. Your champion. The CTO. Because the last activity logged in the CRM is eight days ago, and that was an email you sent, not a reply from her.

[00:06:26] Tom Brennan (AE): Yeah, she's — I think she's been traveling. She mentioned a conference or something.

[00:06:32] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): You think? Or you know?

[00:06:35] Tom Brennan (AE): I — okay, I don't know for sure. She just hasn't responded.

[00:06:39] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Tom, this is the thing that worries me. Your champion has gone dark for over a week, legal has been sitting on this for three weeks, and we don't have clarity on what the actual blocker is. That's not a deal in legal review. That's a deal that might be dying.

[00:06:54] Tom Brennan (AE): I don't think it's dying. They were so engaged. Priya was —

[00:06:58] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): I hear you. But engagement three weeks ago doesn't mean engagement today. People's priorities shift. Budgets get re-evaluated. New leadership comes in and kills projects. We've seen it all.

[00:07:10] Tom Brennan (AE): Yeah. Yeah, you're right.

[00:07:13] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): So here's what I want to do. And I'm not saying this to be hard on you, I'm saying this because a hundred and twenty K is a lot of money to have sitting in limbo. What if we proactively offer a HIPAA compliance audit as part of the project scope?

[00:07:27] Tom Brennan (AE): Like, bake it into the proposal?

[00:07:29] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Exactly. Frame it as, "We understand the compliance concerns, and rather than leaving it to back-and-forth between legal teams, we'll include a dedicated HIPAA compliance audit phase at the start of the project. Our engineering team will work directly with your compliance officer to document every requirement before we write a line of code."

[00:07:48] Sarah Kim (Senior AE): That's smart. It actually de-risks it for them.

[00:07:51] Tom Brennan (AE): Would that change the price?

[00:07:53] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): We could absorb it or add maybe five to ten K. But honestly, if it unsticks a hundred and twenty K deal, it's worth it either way. The point is to give their compliance team a path forward that doesn't feel like a leap of faith.

[00:08:07] Tom Brennan (AE): Okay, I like that. That gives me something concrete to go back with instead of just another "checking in" email.

[00:08:14] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Right. And do not send another email. Call Priya. If she doesn't pick up, call — who else do you have in that organization?

[00:08:24] Tom Brennan (AE): There's David Chen, he's the project lead. He was in most of the meetings.

[00:08:29] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Great. Call David. And call Priya. Today. I want to know by end of day whether this deal is alive or whether we need to adjust the forecast.

[00:08:38] Tom Brennan (AE): Got it. I'll make the calls this morning.

[00:08:41] Leah Ruiz (Sales Ops): And Tom? Can you please update the deal stage in the CRM? It still shows as "Proposal Sent" and it's been twelve days since you updated anything on it.

[00:08:50] Tom Brennan (AE): Yeah, sorry. I'll — yeah, I'll update it today.

[00:08:53] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Thank you, Leah. Okay, while we're on Tom's deals — CloudPeak Ventures. What's happening there?

[00:09:01] Tom Brennan (AE): So CloudPeak, forty-five K, e-commerce rebuild on Shopify Plus. They were — they were really hot three weeks ago. I did the discovery, sent over the proposal, they loved it. The founder, Jake, was super engaged. He was asking about timelines, talking about integration with their fulfillment system, all the right things.

[00:09:20] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): And then?

[00:09:22] Tom Brennan (AE): And then... nothing. I followed up twice. Once last Monday, once on Thursday. No response.

[00:09:29] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Have you tried calling?

[00:09:31] Tom Brennan (AE): I — no, just emails.

[00:09:34] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Tom.

[00:09:35] Tom Brennan (AE): I know, I know. I should've called.

[00:09:38] Sarah Kim (Senior AE): Does anyone at CloudPeak have a connection to us? Like, did they come through a referral or anything?

[00:09:43] Tom Brennan (AE): No, they came inbound through the website. Filled out the contact form.

[00:09:47] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Sarah, do you have any contacts there?

[00:09:50] Sarah Kim (Senior AE): CloudPeak... no, I don't think so. The name doesn't ring a bell. Leah, are they in any of our marketing lists?

[00:09:57] Leah Ruiz (Sales Ops): Let me check. Um... they opened our November e-commerce webinar email but didn't attend. That's the only marketing engagement besides the contact form.

[00:10:08] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Okay, so not a lot of depth there. Tom, what's your gut telling you? What happened?

[00:10:14] Tom Brennan (AE): Honestly? I think either the budget got pulled or they went with someone cheaper. Jake mentioned they'd talked to one other agency. I didn't get the name.

[00:10:24] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Could the champion have left the company?

[00:10:27] Tom Brennan (AE): I didn't think of that. Let me check LinkedIn.

[00:10:30] Leah Ruiz (Sales Ops): I just looked — Jake Morrison is still listed as founder and CEO at CloudPeak. Updated his profile two days ago, actually. So he's still there.

[00:10:39] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Okay, so he's still there, he's just not responding. Tom, here's what I'd do. Don't send a third follow-up email. That's desperate. Send something with value. Like, "Hey Jake, I was putting together some research on Shopify Plus migrations for DTC brands and thought of you. Here's a case study of what we did for" — who's that outdoor brand we did last year?

[00:11:00] Sarah Kim (Senior AE): Summit Trail. The Shopify Plus migration.

[00:11:03] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Right. "Here's what we did for Summit Trail. Tripled their conversion rate in six months." Give him a reason to re-engage.

[00:11:11] Tom Brennan (AE): Yeah, okay. That's better than "just circling back."

[00:11:14] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Way better. Nobody wants to read another "just checking in" email. And again — try to call him. I know everyone hates the phone these days, but a phone call communicates a level of commitment that an email never will.

[00:11:27] Tom Brennan (AE): Fair.

[00:11:29] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Leah, for forecasting purposes, where are we putting CloudPeak?

[00:11:33] Leah Ruiz (Sales Ops): I'd say at risk, at best. Three weeks of radio silence after a proposal — our historical close rate for deals that go dark that long is about fourteen percent.

[00:11:44] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Fourteen percent. Tom, I don't want to kill your optimism, but we should probably not count on CloudPeak in the Q2 forecast unless something changes this week.

[00:11:54] Tom Brennan (AE): That's fair. I'll try to get Jake on the phone today or tomorrow.

[00:11:59] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Good. Okay, Sarah, let's do UrbanGrid quickly. That's the easy one, right?

[00:12:04] Sarah Kim (Senior AE): Yeah, UrbanGrid Properties. Thirty-five K, website plus CMS. Procurement approved it last Wednesday. We're literally just waiting on the signature. Their finance person is supposed to send over the executed contract this week.

[00:12:18] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Any reason to think it won't come through?

[00:12:21] Sarah Kim (Senior AE): No. I talked to Janet — she's our main contact — on Friday. She said it's done, just has to go through their DocuSign workflow, and apparently their CFO is only in the office Tuesdays and Thursdays. So probably tomorrow.

[00:12:34] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Okay. I'm going to count that one as committed. Leah, move that to committed in the forecast.

[00:12:39] Leah Ruiz (Sales Ops): Done. Well, I'll do it after the meeting. Let me just — I'm writing it down.

[00:12:44] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): And Apex Dynamics, Tom. The design sprint deal.

[00:12:48] Tom Brennan (AE): Yeah, this one's looking good, actually. Twenty-eight K marketing site refresh. We're in the middle of a two-week design sprint with them, wraps up Friday. And honestly, they're loving the work. I showed them the homepage concepts last Thursday and their marketing director literally said, "This is exactly what we wanted."

[00:13:06] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): That's great. So what happens Friday?

[00:13:09] Tom Brennan (AE): We present the full design comp — all the key pages — and then the proposal is for us to move into development. I've already got the SOW drafted. If they say yes Friday, we can start dev the following Monday.

[00:13:21] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Love it. Confidence level?

[00:13:23] Tom Brennan (AE): I'd say like, ninety percent? They have budget, they love the work, and the sprint was their idea to begin with. They wanted to test us before committing.

[00:13:32] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Fair enough. Leah?

[00:13:34] Leah Ruiz (Sales Ops): Yeah, the engagement data backs that up. Tom's logged twelve touchpoints in the last two weeks, they've had three internal stakeholders in the design reviews. This one looks solid.

[00:13:46] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Nice. Okay, see, Tom? This is what good deal hygiene looks like. Twelve touchpoints in two weeks, updated CRM. Do that for all your deals and we won't have the Meridian problem.

[00:13:57] Tom Brennan (AE): Yeah, I hear you. I hear you.

[00:14:01] Sarah Kim (Senior AE): To be fair, Apex is a lot simpler than a healthcare portal with HIPAA concerns.

[00:14:06] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): That's true. But the CRM discipline is the same regardless of deal complexity. Anyway — not trying to pile on, Tom. Just want to make sure we're tight across the board.

[00:14:16] Tom Brennan (AE): No, it's all good. Point taken.

[00:14:19] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Alright, Leah, let's talk forecast math. Where do we actually land?

[00:14:24] Leah Ruiz (Sales Ops): Okay, so let me run through it. Committed: UrbanGrid at thirty-five K. That's it for committed. Probable: NovaBuild at eighty-five K, assuming the demo goes well and budget gets pre-approved. Apex at twenty-eight K. So probable total is one thirteen. Committed plus probable is one forty-eight.

[00:14:47] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): And we need two fifty.

[00:14:49] Leah Ruiz (Sales Ops): Right. So we're a hundred and two K short of target on committed plus probable. At-risk we've got Meridian at one twenty and CloudPeak at forty-five, totaling one sixty-five. If both of those come in, we're at three thirteen, well over target. But...

[00:15:07] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): But we can't bank on at-risk deals. That's why they're called at risk.

[00:15:12] Leah Ruiz (Sales Ops): Exactly.

[00:15:14] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): So we've got a hundred K gap. That's significant. And this is — what, week three of Q2?

[00:15:20] Leah Ruiz (Sales Ops): Week four, technically.

[00:15:22] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Week four. Okay. So the question is: where's the pipeline to fill that gap? What early-stage conversations do we have? Sarah, what else are you working on?

[00:15:32] Sarah Kim (Senior AE): I've got two early-stage things. There's a fintech startup called Prism — they're looking at a full web app build, but it's very early. I had one intro call. Could be big, like two hundred K plus, but they're still figuring out their requirements. And then there's a nonprofit, Second Harvest something — they want a website redesign. Probably thirty to forty K range but I haven't done discovery yet.

[00:15:56] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): When are you doing discovery?

[00:15:58] Sarah Kim (Senior AE): I was going to schedule it for next week. After the NovaBuild demo.

[00:16:02] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Can you do it this week?

[00:16:04] Sarah Kim (Senior AE): Uh, yeah. Yeah, I can probably squeeze it in Wednesday or Thursday.

[00:16:08] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Do it. We need pipeline. Even if it closes in Q3, I want it in motion. Tom, what about you?

[00:16:15] Tom Brennan (AE): I've got — honestly, not a lot in early stage right now. I've been focused on Meridian and CloudPeak and the Apex sprint. Let me think... there was a referral that came in from, uh, from the Apex people actually. Some company their marketing director knows. I haven't followed up yet.

[00:16:33] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): A referral you haven't followed up on?

[00:16:36] Tom Brennan (AE): It just came in Friday.

[00:16:38] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Tom, referrals are gold. Follow up today. Today. Before you do anything else, actually. Referrals close at three X the rate of inbound leads.

[00:16:47] Tom Brennan (AE): Yeah, you're right. I'll call them this morning.

[00:16:50] Leah Ruiz (Sales Ops): Can I throw out some data here? So I ran the conversion numbers from last quarter and our discovery-to-close rate was thirty-two percent. And average time from first touch to close was forty-seven days. So if we need a hundred K more in Q2, we need about three hundred K in new pipeline entering discovery in the next two weeks to have a shot at closing it before end of quarter.

[00:17:14] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Three hundred K in new pipeline. That's — I mean, that's a lot.

[00:17:19] Leah Ruiz (Sales Ops): It is. But that's the math.

[00:17:22] Sarah Kim (Senior AE): What if Meridian comes through? That alone covers most of the gap.

[00:17:26] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): It does. And that's exactly why I'm pushing so hard on Tom to unstick it today. If Meridian closes, we're in much better shape. But we can't have our entire quarter riding on one deal that's been dark for three weeks.

[00:17:39] Tom Brennan (AE): It hasn't been dark. Legal's been —

[00:17:41] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Tom. Your champion hasn't responded in eight days. That's dark.

[00:17:46] Tom Brennan (AE): ...Yeah. Okay.

[00:17:49] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): I'm on your side here. I want Meridian to close. A hundred and twenty K would be huge for you, for the team, for the quarter. But I need you to treat it with the urgency it deserves. No more waiting for email replies. Get on the phone.

[00:18:02] Tom Brennan (AE): I will. I got it.

[00:18:05] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Okay. So let's talk about lead gen for a second. Leah, you had an idea about a webinar?

[00:18:12] Leah Ruiz (Sales Ops): Yeah, so I was looking at our lead sources from last quarter, and the webinar we did in November on headless CMS — the one with the Contentful integration walkthrough — that actually generated seventeen qualified leads. Five of them turned into discovery calls, and two became deals. One of which was UrbanGrid, actually.

[00:18:31] Sarah Kim (Senior AE): Oh, really? I didn't know that.

[00:18:33] Leah Ruiz (Sales Ops): Yeah, Janet from UrbanGrid attended that webinar and then filled out the contact form the next week. So it was a direct pipeline generator. I was thinking we could do something similar, maybe focused on e-commerce. Like "Migrating to Shopify Plus: What Enterprise Brands Need to Know" or something.

[00:18:49] Tom Brennan (AE): That could be good, actually. We've got the Summit Trail case study, plus the Apex work, plus — if CloudPeak comes through — that's three Shopify Plus projects we can reference.

[00:18:59] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): I like it. When could we realistically do this?

[00:19:03] Leah Ruiz (Sales Ops): If we start promoting this week, we could run it in two to three weeks. I'd need help from marketing on the landing page and email sequence, but the content itself — one of our engineers could do the technical demo portion, and Sarah or Tom could handle the business case side.

[00:19:19] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Would it generate pipeline fast enough for Q2?

[00:19:22] Leah Ruiz (Sales Ops): Honestly? Probably not for this quarter. The leads would likely close in Q3. But it fills the top of funnel, which we clearly need. And if we don't start now, Q3 is going to look just as tight.

[00:19:35] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): That's fair. Okay, let's do it. Leah, can you put together a one-pager on the webinar plan? Topic, timeline, promotion strategy? I'll take it to marketing this week.

[00:19:45] Leah Ruiz (Sales Ops): Yeah, I can have that by Wednesday.

[00:19:48] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Great.

[00:19:50] Sarah Kim (Senior AE): One thought on the webinar — what if we co-host it with Shopify? We have that partner relationship with their Plus team. Could add credibility and they might promote it to their audience too.

[00:20:01] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Oh, that's a great idea. Leah, add that to the plan. See if we can get Shopify's partner team involved.

[00:20:07] Leah Ruiz (Sales Ops): On it.

[00:20:09] Tom Brennan (AE): Sorry, I need to grab coffee real quick. Can I — does anyone want anything? I'm going to the kitchen.

[00:20:14] Sarah Kim (Senior AE): I'm good, thanks.

[00:20:15] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): I'm fine. Tom, just — we're almost done, can you hold off?

[00:20:18] Tom Brennan (AE): Yeah, yeah. Sorry. I'll survive.

[00:20:22] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Okay, while we're on the topic of pipeline generation — Sarah, your outbound has been pretty strong this quarter. What's working for you?

[00:20:30] Sarah Kim (Senior AE): Honestly, it's mostly personalization. I stopped using the templates a while ago. I'll go look at a prospect's actual website, screenshot specific things that are broken or outdated, and reference those in the email. Like, "Hey, I noticed your checkout flow has six steps and your mobile nav is broken on iOS Safari." That kind of thing.

[00:20:49] Tom Brennan (AE): You screenshot their broken website? That's — isn't that kind of aggressive?

[00:20:53] Sarah Kim (Senior AE): It's direct. And it works. People respond because it shows I actually looked at their stuff instead of just blasting a generic template. My reply rate is like nine percent.

[00:21:03] Leah Ruiz (Sales Ops): Nine point two, to be precise. Tom, yours is one point eight.

[00:21:08] Tom Brennan (AE): Okay, wow, Leah. Just — right for the jugular.

[00:21:11] Leah Ruiz (Sales Ops): I'm just reporting the data.

[00:21:13] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): And the data is telling us something. Tom, I'm not going to mandate you copy Sarah's approach word for word, but a one point eight percent reply rate means your outbound is basically dead weight. You're spending time writing emails that nobody reads.

[00:21:26] Tom Brennan (AE): Yeah, I know. The templates are pretty stale. I've been meaning to rework them but with the sprint and Meridian and everything...

[00:21:33] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): I get it. But this is one of those things where a few hours of investment in better email copy pays dividends for months. Sarah, would you be willing to share a few of your top-performing outbound emails with Tom? Not as templates to copy, but as examples of the approach.

[00:21:47] Sarah Kim (Senior AE): Yeah, totally. I'll drop a few in Slack after this.

[00:21:51] Tom Brennan (AE): That'd be great, actually. Thanks, Sarah.

[00:21:54] Sarah Kim (Senior AE): Of course. And honestly, the biggest thing is just — take five extra minutes per email. Look at their site. Find something specific. It doesn't have to be a screenshot, just show you did your homework.

[00:22:04] Tom Brennan (AE): Makes sense. I've been in quantity mode when I should probably be in quality mode.

[00:22:09] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): Bingo. Okay, let me shift to the overall picture for a second and then we'll wrap up. Leah, pull up the conversion funnel from last quarter. I want everyone to see this.

[00:22:19] Leah Ruiz (Sales Ops): Okay, so last quarter — Q1 — we had forty-two outbound-generated leads enter the funnel. Of those, eighteen became discovery calls. Of those, nine got to proposal stage. Of those nine, we closed four. So our funnel was forty-two leads to four closed deals. That's about a ten percent end-to-end conversion.

[00:22:42] Diana Osei (VP of Sales): And average deal size on those four?

[00:22:44] Leah Ruiz (Sales Ops): Fifty-eight K average. So four deals at fifty-eight K got us to two thirty-two. Which is why we were short last quarter too, by the way.
